claims.label: Definition 1 (Quaternion Algebra)
claims.statement: The quaternion algebra \mathbb{H} is the four-dimensional associative algebra over \mathbb{R} with basis \{1, \mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k}\} satisfying \mathbf{i}^2 = \mathbf{j}^2 = \mathbf{k}^2 = \mathbf{i}\mathbf{j}\mathbf{k} = -1.
claims.notes: This is the standard Hamilton quaternion algebra. The basis multiplication table is: ij=k, jk=i, ki=j, ji=-k, kj=-i, ik=-j.

claims.label: Definition 2 (Quaternionic Qubit)
claims.statement: A quaternionic qubit is a unit quaternion q \in \mathbb{H} with |q| = 1, representing a point on the 3-sphere S^3 \subset \mathbb{R}^4. The quaternionic qubit space is identified with SU(2) via the map q = a + b\mathbf{i} + c\mathbf{j} + d\mathbf{k} \mapsto \begin{pmatrix} a+di & b+ci \\ -b+ci & a-di \end{pmatrix}.
claims.notes: This definition establishes the geometric interpretation of the qubit as a unit quaternion.

claims.label: Theorem 1 (Quaternion–SU(2) Isomorphism)
claims.statement: The group of unit quaternions \mathrm{Sp}(1) = \{q \in \mathbb{H} : |q| = 1\} is isomorphic to SU(2) as a Lie group.
claims.proof_sketch: The isomorphism is given explicitly by the map \phi: q = a + b\mathbf{i} + c\mathbf{j} + d\mathbf{k} \mapsto \begin{pmatrix} a+di & b+ci \\ -b+ci & a-di \end{pmatrix}. One verifies that \phi preserves multiplication and that the image lies in SU(2) since det(\phi(q)) = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 = |q|^2 = 1.

claims.label: Theorem 2 (Double Cover of SO(3))
claims.statement: The unit quaternion group SU(2) \cong \mathrm{Sp}(1) is a double cover of SO(3): there exists a surjective Lie group homomorphism \pi: \mathrm{Sp}(1) \to SO(3) with kernel \{+1, -1\}.
claims.proof_sketch: The homomorphism is given by the adjoint action of Sp(1) on the imaginary quaternions \mathbb{R}^3 \subset \mathbb{H}: for a unit quaternion q and a pure quaternion v, define \pi(q)(v) = q v q^{-1}. This is an orientation-preserving isometry of \mathbb{R}^3, hence an element of SO(3). The kernel is the set of q such that qvq^{-1} = v for all pure quaternions v, which is {±1}.
claims.notes: This theorem has direct implications for phase accumulation: a 4\pi rotation in SO(3) corresponds to a single 2\pi loop in Sp(1), which is the geometric origin of spinor phase.

claims.label: Proposition 1 (Quaternionic X, Y, Z Gates)
claims.statement: The standard Pauli gates X, Y, Z on a quaternionic qubit are represented by right-multiplication by \mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k} respectively (up to global phase), corresponding to \pi-rotations about the x-, y-, z-axes of the Bloch sphere.
claims.proof_sketch: Under the isomorphism \phi of Theorem 1, right-multiplication by \mathbf{i} maps to the Pauli matrix \sigma_x (up to a factor of i), and similarly for \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k}. This follows from direct matrix computation.

claims.label: Proposition 2 (Non-commutativity of Quaternionic Gates)
claims.statement: For any two non-collinear rotation axes \hat{n}_1, \hat{n}_2 \in S^2, the corresponding unit quaternion gates q_1, q_2 satisfy q_1 q_2 \neq q_2 q_1 in general.
claims.proof_sketch: This follows directly from the non-commutativity of \mathbb{H}: since q_1 and q_2 correspond to rotations about non-parallel axes, their SU(2) images do not commute, which is equivalent to the non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication.
claims.notes: This non-commutativity is a feature: it is precisely the non-commutativity exploited for universal quantum computation.

claims.label: Conjecture 1 (Quaternionic Multi-qubit Universality)
claims.statement: A quaternionic quantum computing model that restricts entangling operations to quaternionic tensor products achieves universal quantum computation on a class of problems strictly larger than classical computation but potentially smaller than the full quantum circuit model.
claims.notes: This conjecture will be investigated in subsequent papers in the series. The precise definition of quaternionic tensor product and its computational power remains open.

claims.label: Result 1 (Bloch Sphere Parametrization)
claims.statement: Numerical experiments confirm that the quaternionic parametrization of the Bloch sphere via unit quaternions q = cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2)(n_x\mathbf{i} + n_y\mathbf{j} + n_z\mathbf{k}) correctly reproduces all standard single-qubit gate operations to machine precision.
claims.notes: Validated against the standard matrix representation using numpy and the custom quaternion_gates.py library.
